data.title: FedEx Shares Climb 2% Following Key Trading Signal
data.description:
Understanding the Power Inflow Signal

The Power Inflow alert is a proprietary signal developed and provided by TradePulse. The alert is issued within the first two hours of the trading day, it highlights when there is a significant shift in order flow, specifically indicating that there's been a strong trend toward buying activity. This suggests a high probability of bullish price movement for the rest of the day, making it a potentially strategic and opportune entry point for active traders.

Order flow analytics analyze real-time buying and selling trends by examining the volume, timing, and order size across both retail and institutional traders. These insights offer a more detailed understanding of price behavior and market sentiment for a stock, allowing the trader or institution to make the most informed decision possible.

FDX Performance
At the time of the Power Inflow, FDX was priced at $369.69. Following the signal:
• Intraday High As Of 2:45PM EST: $378.50 (+2.38%)

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Trump Approval Rating Moves Lower After Dow Jones Hits Record 50,000: Voters Aren't Happy About The Economy
data.description:
Record highs for the stock market indexes continue to be a major talking point for President Donald Trump. A voter poll shows that stock market highs aren’t enough to get the president’s approval rating back into positive territory.

Trump’s Approval Rating

A new Morning Consult poll shows Trump with an approval rating of 45% and a disapproval rating of 52%, compared to 46% and 51% two weeks ago, with both numbers getting worse for the president.

The poll shows the following ratings by political affiliation:

  • Republicans: 86% approval, 14% disapproval
  • Democrats: 11% approval, 79% disapproval
  • Independents: 33% approval, 60% disapproval

Surprisingly enough, Trump’s approval rating went up for Democrat and Independent voters, while approval from Republicans was down from 87% two weeks ago.

Trump’s approval rating is near the lowest of his second presidential term and down significantly from the 52% he had when he began his second term last January. Trump’s ratings were 47% and 47% around this time in his first presidential term, which makes him less popular now.

The poll found Trump to have a favorable rating of 44% and unfavorable rating of 53%. The president’s net favorable rating (favorable minus unfavorable) has been negative most of his second term.

Lower Prices Vs. Stock Market Highs

The latest poll was conducted between Feb. 6 and Feb. 9, after the Dow Jones Industrial Average hit a new record above 50,000.

While 70% of voters say health care and cost reduction should be top presidential priorities, barely half believe the president has made them one (48% for health care and 52% for cost reduction).

On health care and the economy, Trump gets approval ratings of 42% and 44% respectively. Trump’s disapproval on these two issues of 49% and 48%, respectively, are the highest disapproval ratings among the items mentioned in the poll.

The poll shows that voters aren’t impressed enough by record-high stock prices and still want lower prices for health care coverage and at the grocery store.

Stock Market Index Prices

The SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF (NYSE:DIA), which tracks the Dow Jones Industrial Average, is up 3.1% year-to-date in 2026 and up 10.8% over the last 52 weeks.

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: 'Shark Tank' Investor Robert Herjavec Says Wealth Can Feel Surprisingly Shallow. 'You Have A Jet And Then You Think What If I Had A Bigger Jet'
data.description:
Robert Herjavec has built companies worth tens of millions, sold one to AT&T (NYSE:T), flown private jets and spent nearly two decades on “Shark Tank.” By most public measures, he has long since “made it.” But according to Herjavec, wealth itself can feel far more hollow than people expect.

Herjavec has been unusually open about how chasing bigger milestones, flashier symbols of success, and constant comparison can quietly drain meaning from even the most successful life.

Don't Miss:

  • Missed Nvidia and Tesla? RAD Intel Could Be the Next AI Powerhouse — Just $0.85 a Share
  • It’s no wonder Jeff Bezos holds over $250 million in art — this alternative asset has outpaced the S&P 500 since 1995, delivering an average annual return of 11.4%. Here’s how everyday investors are getting started.

From Escaping Poverty To Questioning Success

Herjavec’s life began far from luxury. He came to Canada as an 8-year-old immigrant from Croatia with his parents, who arrived by boat with one suitcase, no money, and no ability to speak English. His father had been imprisoned and could never return to their home country. As a child, Herjavec did not feel poor until classmates labeled him that.

“I remember the first day of school and kids picking on me and making fun of me and telling me I’m a poor immigrant,” he said on the “School of Greatness” podcast last year. “Poverty wasn’t my mindset. My mindset was joy.”

That distinction shaped how he approached success. He has said poverty and abundance are learned mindsets, not fixed identities. Hard work, he observed, can keep someone from falling further behind, but it does not automatically result in wealth.

Years later, after founding and selling companies, Herjavec encountered a different kind of discomfort. Speaking about an exchange with billionaire Mark Cuban, his longtime “Shark Tank” co-star, Herjavec explained how comparison crept back into his life.

Trending: Put professional stock research to work in a single ETF — explore Motley Fool Asset Management's factor-based funds.

At a school event, Herjavec joked that he still worked hard so he could one day own a jet bigger than Cuban’s. Cuban looked at him and replied simply, “Which one?”

“I had a great jet, but it wasn’t as big as Mark’s and if I’m being honest, it probably bothered me on some level,” Herjavec said in a 2024 YouTube video. The realization that Cuban owned multiple jets hit him “like a ton of bricks.”

The moment forced him to pause and reassess why he was still pushing. “When I started my company, I didn’t do it for a bigger paycheck,” he said. “I simply did it to pay my mortgage. That was it. That was my entire goal in life.”

In a wide-ranging conversation, Herjavec said that material upgrades eventually stop delivering satisfaction. “There is no car I want to buy or no jet I need to buy or no home I need to buy to fulfill me,” he said. “I want to feel like when I’m on my deathbed that I say I couldn’t have done more.”

That shift toward purpose over possessions came after difficult personal experiences, including a painful divorce and a period of deep depression in 2014. Herjavec has spoken candidly about reaching a point where he questioned his own value, despite having wealth, status, and public success.

If you’re earning over $100,000 and you’re ready to take your money game seriously, Domain Money makes it easy. They offer free sessions with certified financial planners who work with you one-on-one to create a plan that actually fits your goals. It’s built for professionals who want expert advice they can trust.

What ultimately brought him back, he said, was rediscovering joy and purpose. “Without joy there is no life,” Herjavec said on the podcast. “You can spike to success, but if you don’t have joy, it fades.”

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Here's How Much $1000 Invested In Lloyds Banking Group 5 Years Ago Would Be Worth Today
data.description:
Lloyds Banking Group (NYSE:LYG) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 9.23% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 20.92%. Currently, Lloyds Banking Group has a market capitalization of $82.67 billion.

Buying $1000 In LYG: If an investor had bought $1000 of LYG stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $2,585.25 today based on a price of $5.62 for LYG at the time of writing.

Lloyds Banking Group's Performance Over Last 5 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

data.title: Farmer Bros Q2 Earnings Assessment
